4 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A starting point, not the final list.
If you’re new to DSA prep, the confusion isn’t a lack of resources. It’s the opposite — there are too many lists, too many “500 problems you must solve” sheets, and no clear place to actually begin.
So start here instead.
Ten patterns. Four problems each. Forty problems total.
This is not everything you’ll ever need for interviews. It’s a starting point — a way to build the core recognition skills you’re missing right now, so the next 500 problems make sense instead of feeling random.
Why Start With Patterns, Not a Big List
Almost every interview question is a variation of a small number of underlying shapes. A sliding window problem and a two-pointer problem look different on the surface, but the moment you can name which shape you’re looking at, the problem stops being a mystery.
Solving problems one by one, in no particular order, teaches you that one problem. It doesn’t teach you what to do when you see something new — which is exactly what happens in an actual interview.
Learn the shape first. The problem count can come later.
The 10 Patterns to Start With
Two to three problems per pattern is enough to recognize the template. You’re not trying to master each pattern completely here — just enough to know it when you see it again.
1. Sliding Window
Look for: a contiguous subarray or substring that needs to satisfy some condition.

• Longest Substring Without Repeating Characters (3)

• Minimum Window Substring (76)

• Longest Repeating Character Replacement (424)

• Fruit Into Baskets (904)
2. Two Pointers
Look for: a sorted array, or a need to compare elements from both ends inward.

• Container With Most Water (11)

• 3Sum (15)

• Two Sum II — Input Array Is Sorted (167)

• Trapping Rain Water (42)
3. Binary Search
Look for: a sorted (or “sorted enough”) space — including answer spaces, not just arrays.

• Search in Rotated Sorted Array (33)

• Find Minimum in Rotated Sorted Array (153)

• Koko Eating Bananas (875)

• Capacity To Ship Packages Within D Days (1011)
875 and 1011 are the same idea underneath: binary search on the answer, not on the array itself.
4. Hashing + Prefix Sums
Look for: checking existence or counting occurrences faster than brute force.

• Two Sum (1)

• Group Anagrams (49)

• Subarray Sum Equals K (560)

• Longest Consecutive Sequence (128)
5. Monotonic Stack
Look for: needing the “next greater” or “next smaller” element, in either direction.

• Daily Temperatures (739)

• Next Greater Element II (503)

• Largest Rectangle in Histogram (84)

• Online Stock Span (901)
6. Heap (Top-K Problems)
Look for: any mention of “kth,” “top k,” or “closest k.”

• Kth Largest Element in an Array (215)

• Top K Frequent Elements (347)

• K Closest Points to Origin (973)

• Kth Largest Element in a Stream (703)
7. Intervals
Look for: a list of ranges that need merging, inserting, or overlap checks.

• Merge Intervals (56)

• Non-overlapping Intervals (435)

• Insert Interval (57)

• Meeting Rooms (252)
8. BFS/DFS on Grids and Graphs
Look for: connected regions, shortest paths in unweighted graphs, or level-by-level traversal.

• Number of Islands (200)

• Rotting Oranges (994)

• Binary Tree Level Order Traversal (102)

• Clone Graph (133)
9. Trees
Look for: problems phrased recursively — depth, ancestry, path sums.

• Maximum Depth of Binary Tree (104)

• Lowest Common Ancestor of a Binary Tree (236)

• Diameter of Binary Tree (543)

• Binary Tree Maximum Path Sum (124)
10. Dynamic Programming Basics
Look for: the answer at step n depending on answers at smaller steps, where brute force is exponential.

• Climbing Stairs (70)

• House Robber (198)

• Coin Change (322)

• Longest Increasing Subsequence (300)
How to Actually Work Through This
Don’t try to finish it in a weekend.
Two to three problems per pattern, written from memory, then a timed variant. The first problem in a pattern, you’ll look up the approach. By the third, you should be able to write the basic skeleton before you’ve finished reading the problem.
That’s the actual goal here — not memorizing 40 solutions, but recognizing which of the ten shapes a new problem belongs to.
What Happens After This List
Once you can recognize all ten patterns without help, this list has done its job. From there, expand pattern by pattern — more problems in the ones that show up most for your target companies, and new patterns entirely (tries, union-find, backtracking, advanced DP) once the basics stop feeling shaky.
This is a starting point. Treat it like one.
